coredata.dc:title: One step synthesis of Al/N co-doped carbon nanoparticles with enhanced photoluminescence
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Ruan, Fengping
Lv, Ting
Liu, Yanqiang
Deng, Degang
Zhao, Shilong
Wang, Huanping
Xu, Shiqing
coredata.dc:description: In a typical synthesis, luminescent carbon nanoparticles were synthesized via hydrothermal oxidation of glucose in deionized water. Here we reported the photoluminescence of the carbon nanoparticles greatly enhanced when reacted with Al(NO3)3 for the first time, the resulted carbon dots have appropriate spherical morphology, uniform size and good dispersion. The carbon nanoparticles can exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior and emit bright green photoluminescence under UV excitation, which might provide a potential application for carbon dots in other extensive fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Enhancement
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra for A/N CNPs. (b) PL spectra (λ ex=360nm) of CNPs with different doping strategies.
Fluorescence decay profile of the primordial CNPs and Al/N-CNPs.
(a) TEM images and their size distributions for primordial CNPs. (b) TEM images and their size distributions for Al/N-CNPs.
FTIR spectra of primordial CNPs and Al/N-CNPs.

(a) XPS spectra of the primordial CNPs. (b) XPS spectra of Al/N-CNPs. (c) XPS C 1s spectra of primordial CNPs. (d) XPS C 1s spectra of Al/N-CNPs. (e) XPS Al2p 2/3 spectra of Al/N CNPs. (f) XPS N 1s spectra of Al/N CNPs.

head.ce:title: One step synthesis of Al/N co-doped carbon nanoparticles with enhanced photoluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In a typical synthesis, luminescent carbon nanoparticles were synthesized via hydrothermal oxidation of glucose in deionized water. Here we reported the photoluminescence of the carbon nanoparticles greatly enhanced when reacted with Al(NO3)3 for the first time, the resulted carbon dots have appropriate spherical morphology, uniform size and good dispersion. The carbon nanoparticles can exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior and emit bright green photoluminescence under UV excitation, which might provide a potential application for carbon dots in other extensive fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) have attracted considerable attention due to their promising applications in optoelectronic devices, bioimaging and biological labeling [1–6]. In recent years, extensive research has focused on the preparation of CNPs; CNPs can be prepared from various materials using different routes. At present, approaches to preparing CNPs are normally divided into two main groups, top-down and bottom-up strategies. The top down methods depending on the post-treatment of nanocarbons “broken off” from a larger carbon structure, such as graphite, grapheme, carbon nanotube, carbon soot [7–11]. With the bottom-up approaches, CNPs are synthesized via carbonization of appropriate molecular precursors, for example, Candle soot, glucose, critic salts, EDTA salts and so on [12–17]. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CNPs have low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and are eco-friendly. However, despite the alluring optical properties as well as stable chemical advantages [18–21], the brightness of CNPs reported is not that efficient as the best-performing CdSe/ZnS core shell dots [22], which limits the range of practical use of CNPs in many fields. In our work, we discovered that CNPs achieved much higher photoluminescence when adding with Al(NO3)3 for the first time, providing a new potentiality for the applications of CNPs in other fields.

Typically, 1.54g glucose and 0.3001g Al(NO3)3 were dissolved in 80mL of deionized (DI) water under stirring; the solution was then transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and was heated at a constant temperature of 180°C for 24h. The resulting solution was then cooled at room temperature and the up-layer solution containing product was obtained after centrifugation and dialysis. Simultaneously, to better understanding the reason for the high photoluminescent CNPs, parallel experiments were also carried out as follows: (1) pyrolysis of glucose without any doping, (2) with addition of Al(OH)3 but not Al(NO3)3. The corresponding samples were denoted as primordial CNPs, Al-CNPs and Al/N-CNPs.

Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) (Brook Tensor 27) was conducted to detect the chemical identity of CNPs. The size and morphology of the CNPs were examined with a HitachiH-9500 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at room temperature by using a PL3-211-P spectrometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON, America), and a 450W xenon lamp was used as the excitation source. The UV–vis spectra were analyzed with a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–vis spectrometer. The XPS spectra were investigated at a PHI 5000 Versa Probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα as the source.

Fig. 1(a) depicts the UV–vis and PL spectra of Al/N-CNPs (carbon nanoparticles with the addition of Al(NO3)3). The UV–vis spectra showed an absorption peak centered at about 312nm, it is obvious that the emission peak shifted to longer wavelength as the excitation wavelength increased, accompanied with the PL intensity first increases and then decreases; this excitation dependent phenomena have also been referred previously [2,7,18]. A maximum PL emission peak was detected at around 471nm when excited at 360nm, about 10nm red-shift from the primordial CNPs, the luminescence intensity is almost 12 times enhanced than that of the primordial CNPs (see Fig. 1(b)). To further understand the cause for the higher photoluminescence, an Al-CNPs trial was conducted at the same time; however, no obvious luminescence enhancement was found. It was proposed that the high photoluminescence might be attributed to the nitrogen-containing functional groups formed during the hydrothermal treatment which serve as surface passivation [23–26]. The enhanced photoluminescence in CNPs is likely due to the passivated defects on the surface of carbon nanoparticles, which act as excitation energy traps. The most widely accepted mechanism is radiative recombination of excitions [4,7]. The QYs of the primordial CNPs and Al-CNPs (measured at 360nm excitation wavelength using quinine sulfate as a standard (QY=54%) were 1.72% and 1.36%, respectively. However, the observed emission quantum yields for the most bright Al/N-CNPs were much higher than those of the two CNPs and the previous reported CNPs [7,9,12,15,21], up to 25.7%.

Fig. 2 presents the fluorescence decays of the CNPs; the fluorescence decay of Al/N-CNPs is similar to that of the primordial CNPs; the decay trace for the CNPs was fitted using biexponential functions Y(t) based on non-linear least squares analysis

− t /
τ
2
)
where I is the luminescence intensity, A 1 and A 2 are constants, t is the time, and τ 1 and τ 2 are lifetimes for exponential components. The average decay time (τ ⁎) can be determined by the formula:
(2)

τ

1

A

τ
2
)

We calculated the average lifetime (τ ⁎) is 2.71ns and 2.48ns. The fluorescence lifetimes are short, on the other hand, suggesting the possibility of the radiative recombination of the excitons rising to fluorescence [17].

Fig. 3 shows the TEM images of CNPs. TEM images clearly revealed that the CNPs were spherical in morphology and well dispersed with diameters of about 2.75nm and 3.71nm. It is obvious that after passivation the size of Al/N-CNPs increased. From the TEM characterization, we can conclude that the diameter is not the key parameter to evaluate the PL properties of the CNPs, but also ruled by a distribution of different emissive sites [7,16]. The Al/N-CNPs have a lattice spacing of about 0.2238nm that is close to the (100) lattice spacing of graphite, while the primordial CNPs have a lattice spacing of 0.2698nm, similar to the (020) lattice spacing of graphite.

FTIR was used to examine the surface states of CNPs. These CNPs showed largely common characteristics in their spectra (see Fig. 4). The peak at about 3424cm−1 indicated the existence of OH stretching mode; peaks at about 1702cm−1 and 1634cm−1 are assigned to the CO and CC stretching vibrations. The peak at 1200cm−1 indicated the appearance of C–O–C stretching vibrations. Peaks from 1064–1133 belong to C–H stretching vibrations. And that at 1385cm−1 is characteristic of the amide III C–N stretch, suggesting that N atoms had been successfully introduced into the CNPs as a passivation agent which is responsible for the high photoluminescence.

XPS was further performed to provide convincing evidence for the surface states of the CNPs; the result suggested that the composition of the primordial CNPs is mainly carbon and oxygen, while the Al/N-CNPs are composed of carbon and oxygen, as well as a limited amount of nitrogen and Al element (see Fig. 5(a) and (b)), the atomic content of each element: C, 57.51%; O, 38.34%; N, 2.60%; Al, 1.55% , revealing the successful introduction of N and Al elements into the CNPs. Moreover, the C 1s spectrum of the primordial CNPs shows four peaks at 288.8, 286.2, 285 and 284.8eV, which are attributed to CO, C–O, CC, and C–H/graphite C, respectively (see Fig. 5(c)), while the Al/N-CNPs show four peaks at 288.6, 286.2, 285 and 284.8eV, which are attributed to CO/C–N, C–O, CC/C–H/graphite C, and C–H/graphite C respectively (see Fig. 5(d)). Simultaneously, XPS N 1s spectra of the Al/N-CNPs showed the accurate existence of C–N (see Fig. 5(e)), and the Al 2p spectra revealed that Al2O3 is the unique phase of Al element (see Fig. 5(f)). All these results are consistent with the FTIR, suggesting the bright photoluminescence is attributed to the nitrogen-containing functional groups.

In summary, the CNPs were prepared by hydrothermal oxidation of glucose in water. In our work, we find that the luminescence brightness of the CNPs can be highly enhanced with the addition of Al(NO3)3, and the N element proved playing a key role in improving the photoluminescence. The strong photoluminescent Al/N-CNPs demonstrated promising applications in optoelectronic devices and other fields. More in luminescence intensity is being pursed in the future work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant nos. LR14A040002, LQ13E020003 and LZ14F050001, the Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant nos. 61008042, 51272243 and 61370049.
